segments.s204-g177: ز is the preposition 'from/because of' (poetic) introducing the cause phrase 'ز پیکار تو' (from the fight with you). 'پیکار تو' is a genitival phrase 'the fight of/with you' with the possessor after the head noun (noun + pronoun), a typical Persian order. 'کشته و خسته شد' contains two predicates joined by و: 'کشته شد' is a passive construction ('was killed') where شد functions as the auxiliary 'be' and 'کشته' is the past participle; 'خسته شد' is an intransitive/perfective clause 'became weary' (شد here with an adjectival/predicative element). The whole clause places the cause first and then the resultant states.
segments.s204-g178: چنین is a discourse adverb 'thus/so', linking this clause to what precedes. 'رزم ناگاه' places the noun 'رزم' (battle) followed by the adverb 'ناگاه' (suddenly/unexpectedly) — poetic word order where the circumstance can sit between subject and verb. 'پیوسته شد' is 'became continuous/constant' with پیوسته as an adjective/adverbial meaning 'continuous' and شد as the copular verb 'became'; together they mean the fighting suddenly took on a sustained/continuous character.
segments.s204-g179: که is a relative/complementizer 'who/that' introducing the clause. جوید is an archaic 3rd-person-present (literary) 'seeks' (verb after the relative). گذر سوی ایران is the object phrase 'passage toward Iran' (گذر = passage; سوی ایران = toward Iran). کنون is a final adverb 'now'. Word order is literary: relative + verb + object + adverb.
segments.s204-g180: مگر introduces an exception/condition ('unless' / 'except that'). آنک is a demonstrative/relative 'that/which'. جوشد (literary present/subjunctive) here means 'seethes/boils (with feeling)'. ورا is a dative/locative clitic 'in/on him' (i.e. 'in him'). مغز و خون is a poetic collocation 'marrow and blood' (core/inner being and blood). The clause gives the exceptional condition: unless his inner being is stirred.
segments.s204-g181: اگر is the conditional 'if'. نیستی functions as a literary present/past copula 'is not' (here impersonal/third-person: 'is not (grieved)'). رنج is the noun 'grief/suffering/offense' and افراسیاب is the proper name governed by رنج (the grief of Afrasiab). The clause is conditional/elliptical: 'if Afrasiab is not grieved...'.
segments.s204-g182: که introduces the following result/clause ('that'). گردد is literary 3rd-person present/subjunctive of گشت 'turn/become' — here 'his head will turn' or 'will be turned'. سرش is 'his head' with the pronominal suffix ش. زین سخن is an archaic prepositional phrase 'from/at these words' (زین = 'from this/these' + سخن = 'words'); پرشتاب is an adverb 'with haste/quickly'. The clause means his head will be quickly turned by these words.
segments.s204-g183: ترا is the object 'to you' (here split orthographically into ت + را). ت (ـت) is the old 2nd-person enclitic; را acts as the object/accusative marker/enclitic here. بارگی is a noun 'a burden / burden-bearing (office)' in this context. دادمی is a literary past 'I gave' (داد + می is the classical form). ای جوان is the vocative 'O young man' addressing the addressee.
segments.s204-g184: بدان is the imperative 'know this' (ب prefix + دان 'know'). تات is the ethnonym/group name 'the Tats'. بردی is the 2nd-person past 'you carried/took/led' (برد: take/carry/lead). بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon the champion' (بر = against/against; پهلوان = champion). Together: 'Know that you led the Tats against the champion.'
segments.s204-g73: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause meaning “when” or “as” (classical use). بستم is a past-form of بستن used poetically here to mean ‘I set out’ or ‘I make ready’ (an archaic/poetic sense); کنون is an adverb “now.” سوی + لشکر is the prepositional phrase “toward the army” (sوی = toward, followed by the noun لشکر). شوی is the present/subjunctive form of شدن used here as an intransitive predicate meaning roughly “you go/turn” or “you come to be in that place” (addressing the addressee). The sequence is temporal clause (چو + verb) followed by the direction phrase and the resulting verb.
segments.s204-g74: وزین modifies خستگی and here functions as a descriptive modifier ‘this heavy/weighty’ (classical adjective) attached to ‘fatigue’ (خستگی). زود is an adverb “soon/quickly.” بهتر شوی uses بهتر (comparative/adverbial “better”) with شوی (شدن) to mean ‘you will become better’ or ‘you will recover’; the verb is present/subjunctive/optative in classical narrative indicating expected result.
segments.s204-g75: یکی here means ‘one/a certain’ (cardinal/indefinite). تازیانه is the noun ‘whip’ (the object). The cluster written as ب + دین is the fused preposition+demonstrative ‘بدین’ (به + این) in the text meaning ‘in/at this’; grammatically it marks location (‘in this battlefield’). رزمگاه is the noun ‘battlefield/place of combat’ governed by the prepositional phrase.
segments.s204-g76: ز من is a short poetic order ‘from me’ (ز = from, من = me). گم شده‌ست is a perfect/passive construction ‘has become lost’ = ‘is lost/has been lost.’ از پی is a compound prepositional idea meaning ‘in pursuit of/after’ (از + پی), and تاج شاه is the object of that pursuit: تاج = crown, شاه = king. Together the clause says the whip has been lost to the speaker while following/pursuing the king’s crown.
segments.s204-g77: چو again introduces a temporal clause ‘when.’ آن is the demonstrative ‘that/it’ (referring to the whip). بازیابم is first-person present/subjunctive/perfect-form meaning ‘I recover/find it’ (بازیافتن). بیایم is the present/subjunctive of آمدن ‘I come’; in classical Persian this sequence (چو + verb + بیایم) expresses ‘when I do X, I will come.’ برت is the enclitic object ‘to you’ or ‘upon you/for you’ (بر + ت), marking the indirect object of the following verb.
segments.s204-g78: رسانم is a first‑person singular present/subjunctive of رساندن used here with an object implied (‘I will bring/deliver [it]’). به زودی is the adverbial phrase ‘soon’ (به + زودی). سوی لشکرت is the directional phrase ‘to your army’ (سوی = toward, لشکرت = your army), completing the idea ‘I will bring it to you at/near your army.’ Word order is verb + adverb + direction phrase.
segments.s204-g79: وزانجا is a fused classical adverb meaning ‘and/from there’ or ‘thence’ (وز = and/from + انجا = there), marking the starting point. سوی قلب لشکر is the direction phrase ‘toward the heart/core of the army’ (سوی = toward, قلب = heart/core, لشکر = army). شتافت is a past tense verb ‘he hastened/rushed’ (شتافتن) describing sudden movement toward that point.
segments.s204-g80: همی here is a classical iterative/progressive particle meaning ‘kept on’ or marking continuous action. جست is the past form of پریدن/جستن ‘he leapt’ (here ‘he jumped/ran about’). تا introduces a limit/purpose clause ‘until,’ followed by تازیانه (the object ‘the whip’) and بیافت (present/subjunctive of افتادن/افتادن in the sense ‘to come upon/find’). Together: he kept leaping until he came upon the whip.
segments.s204-g25: This clause states existence/identity with the copula است at the end (SOV word order common in older Persian: subject 'یکی شوشه زر' then location/qualifier 'به سیم اندر' and finally the verb 'است'). اندر is an archaic preposition meaning 'in/within'; به ... اندر here marks that silver is inside the gold tassel. Note the simple present/past-free copular use of است to predicate the whole phrase.
segments.s204-g26: The clause follows the same pattern (subject first). دو شیبش means ‘its two (parts/strands)’ where the pronominal clitic -ش marks possession. ز introduces the material/source ('from/of'), listing materials after it; the final گوهر‌ست is a verblike nominal predicate ('is jewel'). The short connective وز stands between the two listed materials — an archaic conjunction here.
segments.s204-g27: چون here introduces a temporal clause ('when' / 'as'), and the verb بگشاد is the past form with the verbal prefix بـ (a common older/poetic past). Word order places the subordinate temporal clause first: 'فرنگیس چون گنج بگشاد' = 'when Farangīs opened the treasure'. سر follows to mark the item (see next segment where سر combines with 'داد').
segments.s204-g28: This is the main clause: داد is the past-tense verb 'gave' and مرا را is the recipient/object marker construction (here the data shows a split 'م' + 'را'); چنان qualifies the quantity ('so much/such'), followed by the nouns سلیح 'weapons' and کمر 'belt' joined by و. Word order is verb-final (object(s) before the verb 'داد' in the poetic line).
segments.s204-g29: The subject pronoun من begins the clause. برداشتم is the past tense (first person singular) formed on برداشتن 'to take up' (note the common poetic past form). آن درع و تازانه lists the direct objects ('that armor and the lance/weapon'); تازانه here is a nominal item paired with درع.
segments.s204-g30: به + place name (توران) marks direction/location 'to/into Turan'; دگر خوار qualifies what the speaker did with them ('made them despicable/left them as spoil'), and the verb بگذاشتم is past tense 'I put/left' with the verbal prefix بـ typical of classical/poetic Persian. The clause follows the expected object–adverb–verb ordering of the poem.
segments.s204-g31: This clause has an indefinite subject marker یکی 'one' and the adverb نیز 'also'; بخشید is the past tense 'bestowed/gave' (here used transitively) and کاوس شاه is the agent/possessor 'King Kāvus' in apposition. Word order puts the giver after the verb in this poetic line, but syntactically it's 'Kay Kāvus the king also gave one (item)'.
segments.s204-g32: This is an adjectival/nominal description of the gift: ز زر 'of gold' (ز as poetic 'from/of'), وز (here an archaic connector) links the two materials, گوهر 'gem/jewel' names the second material, and the simile چو تابنده ماه ('like the shining moon') modifies the appearance. چو functions as 'like/as' and تابنده is the present-participial adjective 'shining'.
segments.s204-g9: This is a compact nominal clause: 'نبشته بران چرم' = 'inscription on that leather' (noun + postpositional modifier), followed by 'نام منست'. In classical/poetic Persian word order the locative/qualifier ('بران چرم') appears immediately after 'نبشته'. 'نام منست' states whose inscription it is; 'منست' here functions as a predicative/archaic form related to 'من' (possessive sense) or 'منس' (fitting), producing the sense 'is my name / my name is on it' in context.
segments.s204-g10: 'سپهدار' is the subject/title (the commander). 'پیران' is genitive/qualificative here ('of the elders' or 'the elders' as the group). 'بگیرد' is the present-subjunctive/relative-present form meaning 'takes' or 'will take' (poetic present used for future/gnomic sense). 'به دست' is a locative/idiomatic complement 'into the hand' = 'in hand / in his charge'.
segments.s204-g11: The clause begins with the subjunctive/jussive 'شوم' (literally 'may I become') marking a wish or optative. Adjectives/adverbs 'تیز و تازانه' qualify that change — 'sharp/quick and vigorously' — and the verb 'بازآورم' (1st person present/subjunctive) means 'I bring back / I retrieve'. Poetic inversion places the auxiliary wish verb first and the action verb at the end.
segments.s204-g12: A conditional clause headed by 'اگر' ('if/even if'). 'چند' here is an approximative/pluralizing quantifier ('some/many'), modifying 'رنج' ('hardship, suffering'); 'دراز' is the adjective 'long'. 'آورم' is 1st person present/subjunctive or aorist-like poetic form 'I bring/undergo', so the whole line reads 'even if I bring/undergo some long hardships.'
segments.s204-g13: Poetic word order and clitic placement: 'مرا' (here split in the word list) is the dative/object 'to me', fronted for emphasis. 'این' = 'this', 'ز اختر' = 'from a star' (z = 'from'), 'بد آید' = 'an ill/evil comes' (بد as adjective 'bad/unfavourable', آید = 'comes/befalls'), and 'همی' is an emphatic/continuous particle common in classical Persian. The clause means 'this bad thing from a star befalls me.'
segments.s204-g14: 'که' introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ('that / namely'). 'نامم' is 'نام' + possessive suffix 'ـم' = 'my name' (split in the word list); 'به خاک اندر آید' is a fixed poetic phrase meaning 'comes into the earth / is cast into the dust' (به + خاک + اندر + آید), and 'همی' again emphasizes the statement. Grammatically this is a nominal clause explaining the content of the misfortune.
segments.s204-g15: 'بدو گفت' is the light-verb pattern 'he said to him' (bedo = 'to him'); 'گودرز' is the speaker's name (subject). 'پیر‌ای پسر' are vocatives: 'پیر' (elder) with the exclamatory vocative particle 'ای' and 'پسر' (son/youth) — classical Persian often strings vocatives for address. Word order places the indirect object marker before the reporting verb.
segments.s204-g16: 'همی' here is a classical emphatic/presentive particle attached to the clause. 'بخت خویش' = 'one's fortune' (بخت = luck/fate, خویش = reflexive 'one's own' — split in the word list with the possessive clitic). 'اندر آری' is a verbal sequence: 'آری' functions as the present/2nd/3rd-person poetic form meaning 'you bring/you put', with 'اندر' giving the directional/locative sense 'into'. 'به‌سر' ('to an end' / 'utterly') is an adverbial complement. Overall: 'you will bring your fortune utterly to an end.'
segments.s204-g113: سواران همه (subject + quantifier) precedes the verb: 'سواران' (horsemen/riders) + 'همه' (all). 'بازگشتند' is 3rd person plural past tense meaning 'they returned/withdrew.' 'ازوی' is the preposition 'از' + enclitic pronoun 'وی' (poetic form of 'او'), meaning 'from him/her' and marks the source of withdrawal.
segments.s204-g114: بنزدیک is an adverb meaning 'near' or 'drawing near' and modifies the following noun. 'پیران' here functions as the (proper) target toward whom they turn — the name Piran in the narrative (the options given list 'elders' but here it is a person). 'نهادند' is the 3rd person plural past of نهادن 'to place/set' and in the idiom 'نهادند روی' it means 'they set/turned (their) face(s).' 'روی' (face) completes the idiom 'نهادند روی' = 'they turned their faces toward...'.
segments.s204-g115: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' 'لشکر' is the subject 'army/host.' The preposition-like z (ز) with the name 'بهرام' marks relation/possession ('of Bahram' / 'Bahram's'). 'شد' is the past auxiliary 'became' and combines with the adjective 'ناپدید' to yield 'became vanished' = 'disappeared.' The clause reads 'when Bahram's host became vanished.'
segments.s204-g116: ز هر سو is a fixed expression 'from every side/direction' ('ز' = from, 'هر' = every, 'سو' = side/direction). 'بسی' is an adverb of quantity (here functioning as 'many / a great many'). 'تیر' is the noun 'arrows.' 'گرد' functions adverbially/participle-like with 'آورید' to form 'گردآورید' meaning 'they gathered/collected' and 'آورید' is the 3rd person plural past of آوردن 'to bring/collect.' The whole clause: 'they gathered many arrows from every side.'
segments.s204-g117: چو = 'when.' 'لشکر' is the subject 'the army.' 'بیامد' is past tense 'came.' 'بر' is the preposition 'upon / against / to' and links to 'پهلوان' (the champion) as the object of arrival/approach. The clause reads 'when the army came upon/to the champion.'
segments.s204-g118: بگفتند is 3rd person plural past 'they said/spoke.' با او here means 'to him' (i.e., they addressed him). سراسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely' or 'throughout.' گوان is an archaic adverb meaning 'thus' or 'in this manner.' Together the phrase indicates they spoke to him and related the matter fully/so: 'they told him everything thus.'
segments.s204-g119: فراوان سخن رفت is an idiomatic passive-like phrasing: 'فراوان' (abundant) + 'سخن' (talk) + 'رفت' (went) = 'much was talked / much talk went forth.' 'زان' is 'about that / from that' introducing the topic, and 'رزمساز' is a compound noun 'maker/producer of battle' used as 'warrior/battle‑leader.' So: 'much was said about that warrior.'
segments.s204-g120: ز پیکار او = 'of his battle(s)' ('ز' = concerning/about, 'پیکار' = battle, 'او' = his). 'آشکارا' means 'openly/plainly' and 'و راز' pairs 'and secretly' (راز = secret); the contrast marks that his deeds were spoken of both publicly and in private. Grammatically this is a noun phrase expanding on what was said about the warrior.
segments.s204-g49: "همی" is a durative/emphatic particle placed before the verb meaning "continually/was continually"; "زار بگریست" is a fixed poetic expression where "زار" (an adverb/verb of lament) qualifies the verb and together mean "wailed/lamented"; "بگریست" is past tense 3rd‑person singular "he wept". The prepositional phrase "بر کشتگان" (preposition + plural noun) means "over/on the slain" and functions as the object of the lament.
segments.s204-g50: "بران" here is a prepositional/locative particle meaning "on/over them"; "داغ دل" is a compound meaning "the burn/wound of the heart" (داغ as emotional wound/grief + دل "heart"). "بخت‌برگشتگان" is a nominative plural epithet describing those affected (literally "those whose bākh(t) has turned") and links as the possessors/recipients of that "داغ دل." The whole phrase modifies the preceding lament: he lamented the heart‑wound on those whose fortunes had changed.
segments.s204-g51: "تن ریونیز" is a nominal phrase (تن = "body") with an epithet/identifier "ریونیز" (an archaic or proper epithet here). "اندران" is a locative particle meaning "in/among them/therein," and it introduces the locative phrase "خون و خاک" ("blood and dust/earth"). Word order is noun + locative phrase: 'the bodies, in blood and dust...'.
segments.s204-g52: "شده" is the perfect/passive participle meaning "have become/are made" and introduces a resultant state; the predicates that follow describe that state: "غرق" (here passive/participial sense "submerged/overwhelmed/drenched") and "خفتان" (literally "sleeping", used euphemistically for "fallen/dead"). The sequence "برو چاک چاک" is idiomatic/poetic: "برو" is an older verbal/particle element and with the repeated noun "چاک چاک" ("gash, torn") yields the sense "torn to pieces" — a vivid, echoic reduplication common in poetry.
segments.s204-g53: Same structure as earlier: "همی" marks continuous/emphatic past; "زار بگریست" = "wailed/lamented" (زار qualifying the verb); the subject "بهرام شیر" (proper name + epithet "the Lion") follows the verb, a common poetic order (verb before subject).
segments.s204-g54: "که" here introduces the cry/exclamation that follows (functioning like "saying/crying:"), and "زار، ای جوان سوار دلیر" is a vocative/exclamatory phrase: "زار" as an interjection "Alas!" plus the vocative particle "ای" addressing "جوان سوار دلیر" ("young, brave rider"). Adjective order in the vocative is poetic (جوان before سوار; دلیر as final epithet).
segments.s204-g55: "چو" is a subordinating particle meaning "when/since/now that" introducing the circumstance ("چو تو کشته اکنون" = "now that you are slain"); word order places the verb/adjective "کشته" (slain) after the pronoun. "چه یک مشت خاک" is an interrogative/exclamatory noun phrase "what (is) a single handful of earth" — "چه" is the interrogative "what" and "یک مشت خاک" is the quantified noun phrase "a handful of earth."
segments.s204-g56: "بزرگان به ایوان" is a prepositional/locative phrase roughly "(for) the great ones/nobles in the hall/porch" (بزرگان = "the great ones", به ایوان = "at/to the iwan/porch/hall"). The comma marks a contrastive turn: "تو اندر مغاک" is a plain locative clause "you are in the pit/abyss" (تو as subject + اندر مغاک "in the abyss"). The two halves contrast the ceremonial handful of earth given among nobles with the actual fate: being in the abyss.
segments.s204-g225: جدا شد is a past intransitive phrase 'was separated/was torn off' with the verb before the rest for poetic effect. ز تن ('from the body') is a poetic form of از + تن and marks the source; دست خنجر‌گزار is a single noun phrase 'the dagger-wielder's hand' (دست = 'hand', خنجر‌گزار = 'dagger-bearer'). The order places the result verb first then the source and the nominal phrase describing what separated.
segments.s204-g226: فروماند is a past-state verb meaning 'was worn out / gave out'; here it describes the man's exhausted condition. از رزم ('from the battle') gives the cause. و برگشت کار uses برگشت (past of برگشتن, 'turned/was reversed') with کار ('affair, the course of events') to mean that the situation or course of the fight turned around.
segments.s204-g227: The phrase places the agent/name تژاو before the object to show whose feelings these are; ستمگاره را is the definite object marked by را. دل بسوخت is a fixed verbal expression 'his heart burned' (بسوخت = 'burned/was consumed') indicating a strong emotional reaction toward the marked object.
segments.s204-g228: به کردار = 'in the manner/shape of' (کردار here 'manner/behavior'); آتش رخش is an apposition 'the fire of Rakhsh' (رخش = the steed), and برفروخت is a poetic past meaning 'was kindled/was set ablaze' (lit. 'lit up'). Together the clause says that, like fire, Rakhsh was inflamed/kindled.
segments.s204-g229: بپیچید is the past of پیچیدن 'to turn' with the b-prefix common in literary Persian; ازو ('from him') marks the direction/source of the turning. روی ('face') is the noun that is turned, modified by پر + (درد و شرم) meaning 'full of pain and shame' (پر used as 'full (of)'). The clause literally: 'he turned his face away from him, full of pain and shame.'
segments.s204-g230: بجوش آمدش is a compound verbal idea: بجوش ('to boil/seethe') + آمدش ('came to him' with the clitic -ش 'to him') = 'it boiled up in him.' در جگر ('in the jigar/liver') is an idiomatic interior location for feelings, خون گرم ('hot blood') characterizes the blood as heated/fervent. The whole means 'hot blood boiled up in his liver' (i.e. he seethed with emotion).
segments.s204-g81: میان is a locative preposition meaning 'among/within' placed before the phrase. تل کشتگان is a noun + modifier sequence 'mound (of) the slain' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe); اندرون is a postpositive adverb reinforcing location 'inside/within'. The whole chunk gives a circumstantial location for what follows.
segments.s204-g82: برآمیخته is a past participle used predicatively ('mingled') and there is no copula — a common epic compression: 'X mingled'. خاک بسیار و خون lists the mixed substances; بسیار functions as a quantifier 'much/plentiful' modifying the nouns. و is the simple coordinating 'and'.
segments.s204-g83: فرود آمد is a compound past tense meaning 'he descended/ alighted' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از باره آن is a prepositional/phrasing 'on that occasion / because of that' (باره = 'occasion/instance', آن = demonstrative). برگرفت is a simple past verb 'he took up/ seized' whose direct object is omitted in the compressed epic style.
segments.s204-g84: وزانجا is a fused sequence و + زانجا 'and from there'. خروشیدن appears as averbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring / to roar' functioning as the subject of گرفت. اندر گرفت is an idiomatic combination meaning 'took hold' or 'began' — so the clause means 'and from there a roar took hold / began'.
segments.s204-g85: خروش دم مادیان is a compact nominal phrase: خروش 'cry/roar', دم here 'moment/at the breath' (i.e. 'the mare's sudden cry'), مادیان identifies the source 'the mare'. یافت is a past verb 'reached / came to', and اسپ is the direct object 'horse' — so literally 'the mare's cry (at that moment) reached the horse.'
segments.s204-g86: بجوشید is a past intransitive verb here meaning 'boiled/was agitated' (figurative of excitement or restiveness). برسان is used as a verb indicating movement/goal 'bring/bring to / reach', with the proper name آذرگشسپ as its object; the subject (the horse) is implied. The clause compresses 'the horse became agitated and rushed toward Azar‑Gushasp.'
segments.s204-g87: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. مادیان is the goal 'the mare'. روی بنهاد is a past-tense verbal phrase 'he set/laid (his) face' i.e. 'he turned his face' (روی = face; بنهاد = he placed/set). تفت here is a terse poetic modifier following the verb; the literal sense 'to scorch/burn' is used figuratively as an intensifier (common in epic diction) modifying the manner.
segments.s204-g88: غمی گشت is a small verbless-predicate construction: غمی (grief) is the predicate and گشت ('became') is the copular verb — 'became grief' = 'became sorrowful'. بهرام is the subject. و از پس برفت is a conjunctive sequence 'and after that he departed' (از پس = 'after', برفت = 'he went away'); note برفت here is an archaic past form meaning 'departed'.
segments.s204-g1: دوان رفت is a poetic compound of manner + verb meaning “ran/rushed”; the verb رفت is simple past (he went/ran). The following noun بهرام is the subject (Bahram), and پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase “before/in the presence of his father” (پیش + noun) indicating the goal of his rushing.
segments.s204-g2: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/intensifier introducing direct address (classical spelling combines که + ای); پهلوان یلان سر به سر is a string of epithets: پهلوان “champion”, یلان “heroes”, and the idiomatic pair سر به سر “head to head / equal / face to face”. Word order places the vocative/description after the movement to the father, as in archaic/narrative Persian.
segments.s204-g3: بدانگه is an adverbial time marker “thereupon/at that moment.” The conjunction/particle که introduces the content clause that follows. آن تاج برداشتم is a complete action: آن تاج (that crown) + برداشتم (first‑person past 'I took/removed'), with the subject ‘I’ implied by the verb ending.
segments.s204-g4: به نیزه is a prepositional phrase indicating location/instrument ('onto/on a spear'); بابراندر is an archaic/verbal element linked to the following verb, here functioning as part of the compound action, and افراشتم is first‑person past “I raised/exalted.” Together they mean literally ‘I put (it) on a spear and raised it up.’
segments.s204-g5: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain' here introducing an item among others; تازیانه is the object 'whip.' The possessive relation is expressed by ز من 'from me' (poetic/genitive), and گم شده‌ست is a perfect/resultative construction 'has become lost / has been lost' (third person singular).
segments.s204-g6: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' گیرند is third‑person plural present (or general present) 'they seize/take.' ترکان is the agent 'the Turks,' modified by the adjective بی‌مایه 'worthless/mean.' The phrase به دست literally 'into the hand(s)' functions idiomatically as 'into the hands of,' so the clause means 'when the worthless Turks seize (it) into their hands.'
segments.s204-g7: به بهرام is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to/for Bahram.' بر چند باشد is a rhetorical question: بر چند (چند = 'how much / to what degree') with باشد (a present/subjunctive/optative form) giving 'how much would ... be/effect.' فسوس is a small, insidious whisper/idle whispering; together the clause asks rhetorically how much such whispers would affect Bahram.
segments.s204-g8: جهان پیش چشمم is a locative/experiential phrase 'the world before my eyes' (پیش + چشم + -م = 'my eye'). شود is third‑person singular 'becomes,' and آبنوس is a predicate adjective 'ebony/very dark.' The clause means 'the world becomes (as) ebony before my eyes,' i.e. the world grows dark to me.
segments.s204-g217: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). قلم شد is a past construction: قلم (literally 'pen') + شد ('became') = 'became a pen', used metaphorically. به گرز و به تیغ is a prepositional phrase ('to/against the mace and the sword') that names the things with/against which the spear was compared.
segments.s204-g218: همی before a past verb gives a durative/continuous sense ('kept / was continually'); چکانید is the past of 'to drip/let fall drops'. بر marks location/target ('upon'), and تیره میغ is an adjective + noun ('dark blade'), with تیره qualifying میغ.
segments.s204-g219: چو marks a temporal clause ('when'). رزمش is 'his battle/fighting' (رزم + enclitic -ش). برین گونه is an archaic phrase corresponding to بر این گونه ('in this manner'), here meaning 'thus'; پیوسته شد is 'became continuous' (پیوسته 'continuous' + شد 'became').
segments.s204-g220: به تیر‌ش is a prepositional phrase indicating cause/means ('by/through his arrow(s)'); -ش on تیر is the third‑person clitic 'his/its'. دلاور is the noun 'hero' (subject). بسی is an intensive/adverbial 'very/much' here modifying خسته ('very tired'), and شد is the past copula 'became'.
segments.s204-g221: چو 'when' introduces the clause. بهرام is the proper name Bahram; یل گشت is 'became a hero' (یل 'hero' + گشت 'became'). بی‌توش و تاو are two nouns/adjectival items joined by و; these are archaic/poetic attributes (بی‌توش is obscure/archaic, تاو 'radiance/glow').
segments.s204-g222: پس 'then/after' signals sequence. پشت او 'his back' (پشت + pronoun او). اندر آمد is a classical Persian verbal phrase 'came in/arrived upon', and تژاو (here placed after the verb for emphasis) is the thing that arrived — 'radiance/brightness' came upon his back.
segments.s204-g223: یکی here is an indefinite 'one/someone'. تیغ زد is the verb phrase 'struck with a sword' (تیغ 'sword' + زد 'struck'). بر سر کتف marks the precise target ('on the top/head of the shoulder'), and اوی is the enclitic possessive here ('his').
segments.s204-g224: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/whereupon'). شیر 'lion' is the subject; اندر آمد is 'came in/arrived' (classical phrase), ز بالا 'from above' gives direction, and به روی means 'onto the face' (به marking direction toward + روی 'face').
segments.s204-g41: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic adverbial particle meaning 'now/at this moment' placed after the conditional. تازانه is an adverb 'freshly/anew' modifying the verb. بازآورم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative (آوردن with the -م ending) 'I bring back / I could restore' — together the clause means 'If now I could restore (him) anew.'
segments.s204-g42: وگر = 'or if' (alternative conditional). سر is the direct object 'head'. ز کوشش is a prepositional phrase 'from/by effort' giving cause or means. بگاز آورم is a compound where بگاز (from گاز زدن) 'to bite/tear' plus آورم 'I bring' is used idiomatically in classical Persian to mean 'to bring (off) by/with a biting/torn action' — here in first-person conditional sense 'I could (bring/tear) off the head by striving'. The verb phrase is conditional because of وگر/گر.
segments.s204-g43: بر او = 'toward him / upon him' (بر used here as 'toward'). رای یزدان is a genitive noun phrase 'the counsel/plan of God' (رای governed by یزدان). دگرگونه بود is the predicate 'was different' (دگرگونه + بود, past copula). The word order places the target (بر او) before the subject phrase رای یزدان, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s204-g44: همان = 'that same / the very'. گردش بخت is a genitive construction 'the turning/cycle of fate' with گردش as head and بخت as its qualifier. وارونه = 'inverted, reversed' is the predicate adjective, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The line states that the wheel/turning of fortune was turned upside-down.
segments.s204-g45: هرانگه که = 'whenever / at the time when' (temporal subordinating phrase). بخت اندر آید = 'fortune enters / comes in' with اندر = 'into/in' preceding the verb (classical placement). به خواب = 'into sleep' or 'to sleep' (به marks direction/entry into the state خواب). The whole clause is a temporal subordinate: 'whenever fate goes to sleep.'
segments.s204-g46: ترا (here tokenized as ت + را) = 'to you' / 'you' as the indirect object (ت = 2nd-person suffix, را = object/definite marker forming ترا). گفت here functions as 'said' (poetic/inverted word order places گفت before the subject), دانا = 'the wise (one)' (subject), نیاید = present negative 'does not come' / 'will not come', and صواب = 'rightness / good outcome / remedy'. The clause reports what the wise told you: 'the wise said to you that no good/right will come.' Note the inversion (گفت دانا) is a poetic order.
segments.s204-g47: بزد is the past tense 'he struck / he smote / he spurred'. اسپ = 'steed / horse' (object). و آمد = 'and came'. بران = 'to that / upon that' (بر + آن contracted), indicating direction toward the following noun. رزمگاه = 'battlefield' (place). Word order is verb–object then conjunction then motion verb plus place phrase.
segments.s204-g48: درخشان شده is a participial/perfect construction 'become shining' (درخشان + شده, 'has become bright'). روی گیتی = 'the face/surface of the world' (روی = face, modified by گیتی). ز ماه = causal/agent phrase 'because of / from the moon' (ز used poetically as 'because of' or 'by'). The clause means the world's face grew bright by the moon's light.
segments.s204-g17: ز بهر is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of' (ز here used in the sense 'because of' + بهر 'for'). یکی چوب is a noun phrase 'one/a certain stick' (یکی = 'a certain/one', چوب = 'stick'); بسته‌دوال is a poetic/compound adjective attached to چوب and here left untranslated as a rare/obscure epithet (it modifies the stick). Word order is preposition + purpose phrase followed by the quantified noun phrase.
segments.s204-g18: شوی is the 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن used here as 'you become' / 'you may become' (optative nuance). در دم is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the moment', دم functioning as 'moment'. اختر is the temporal/astral noun 'star'. شوم‌فال is a predicate adjective 'ill‑omened' (lit. 'of bad omen'); the clause predicates that state of being on the subject implied by the previous phrase.
segments.s204-g19: چنین گفت is the common narrative formula 'thus said'; گفت here functions as the past‑tense speech verb 'said' and introduces direct speech. بهرام جنگی is a name with epithet ('Bahram, the warrior') in apposition; که begins the reported clause 'that' (introducing what he said). Word order places the speech formula before the reported clause.
segments.s204-g20: نیم here is an archaic negative/existential-like particle attached to the predicate meaning 'I am not' (classical usage different from the literal 'half'); بهتر از = 'better than' with از functioning as the comparative marker. دوده و انجمن are the items compared to (literally 'smoke and the assembly'); the clause asserts the speaker's humility by denying superiority over them.
segments.s204-g21: به جایی توان is an interrogative/mode cluster: به جایی 'to/into any place' placed before the modal auxiliary توان 'can' (classical Persian often places the place phrase before the auxiliary). مرد is the subject 'a man', and کاید زمان is a noun phrase 'the plot/cunning of Time' (کاید governs زمان: 'Time's plotting'). The segment sets up the possibility question about a man's ability in the face of Time's machinations.
segments.s204-g22: به کژی literally 'to crookedness/wrongness' (به + noun) expresses direction/result. چرا is the interrogative 'why'. برد here is used as a verb/noun with a meaning not given in the supplied sense list (the line asks why suspicion should 'bring' or 'lead to' something), so its sense is marked unknown; باید گمان is a clause 'should [there be] suspicion' or 'must one suppose' (باید = 'must/ought', گمان = 'suspicion/conjecture'). The whole is a rhetorical question: 'Why should one suppose [that] crookedness results?'.
segments.s204-g23: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said' — here گفت is the speech verb introducing direct address). گیو‌ای is the vocative 'Giv' (with vocative -ای), and برادر مشو is an admonition 'do not be [so], brother' (مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become / do not be'). The segment is direct speech addressing Giv.
segments.s204-g24: فراوان مرا is a phrase meaning 'many/much for me' or 'I have many' (فراوان as adverb/adjective 'abundant', مرا = 'for me' / 'to me' here). م (lemma #هست) stands for the verb 'is/there is' (existential), and را is the object/ topicalizing marker attached in the line that in speech sometimes emphasizes the following noun. تازیانه‌ست is a predicative compound 'is a whip/whips' (تازیانه 'whip' + ـست 'is'), and نو is 'new'. Together the clause conveys that many new whips exist/await the speaker ('there are many new whips for me' or 'I have many new lashes').
segments.s204-g89: همی is a durative adverb (here 'kept / continuously') preceding the verb. شد is the past tense of شدن and here marks the past action (literally 'became / went on'); together همی شد conveys 'kept on / continued'. دمان (poetic spelling of دامن) is a noun 'skirt / slope' and is the thing being traversed; تا introduces the limit 'until' and is followed by رسید (past 'reached'). اندروی is an adverbial/locative 'into the inner part / inward' modifying رسید. Word order is verb-final for the clause's main verb رسید, with the adverbial phrase (همی شد دمان) placed before the until-clause.
segments.s204-g90: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ترگ and خفتان are nouns joined by و ('and'); here they name things/sources (literary/poetic items) and are followed by the adjective phrase پر از آب روی. پر ('full') takes the following از + noun to mean 'full of', so پر از آب = 'full of water'. روی here means 'surface/face' (a locative/visual specification). The whole fragment describes the surface being 'full of water' coming from/with those things named.
segments.s204-g91: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. بگرفت is past-tense simple (بـ- prefix + گرفت) meaning 'he seized / grasped'. هم here means 'at the same time / together' (emphasizing simultaneity) and combines with در زمان ('in that moment') to mark synchronic action. برنشست is a past form (بر + نشست) meaning 'sat up / rose up'; the clause order is temporal (چو + verb) followed by the concurrent action.
segments.s204-g92: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)' and functions as the subject. تیغ هندی is a noun phrase 'Indian sword' (تیغ = sword; هندی = Indian / Indian‑style adjective). گرفته is a perfect/participle form 'taken / having taken' describing the subject's state; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand' where به is the preposition and دست is the noun. The segment is a compact verbless description using a participle (گرفته) to indicate possession of the sword.
segments.s204-g93: چو is again 'when'. بفشارد is an old/stylistic present-subjunctive or jussive form (here used in reporting past habitual/action-in-background) of فشردن with the prefix بـ-; in context it functions as a past narrative 'he pressed / urged'. ران is the object 'thigh' (i.e., urging the horse). هیچ is the negative 'no/none' and نگذارد is past-tense negative of گذاردن used here as 'let / allow' (he allowed none). پی is a postpositional/relational noun 'after / following' indicating 'after him' or 'to follow him'. Word order places the conditional/temporal clause first and the negated result after it.
segments.s204-g94: This is a nominal apposition listing those left behind or affected: سوار 'rider' و 'and' تن 'body/person'. باره (here 'بارۀ' meaning 'bearing a load') modifies تن to mean 'load‑bearing person' or 'baggage‑carrier'. پرخاک is a compound adjective 'dusty' ('full of dust'), and the final و خوی links another attributive 'and (of) disposition / habit' (خوی used adjectivally/idiomatically). The string is noun + modifiers in compact poetic order without an explicit verb.
segments.s204-g95: چنان 'so / thus' modifies the degree expressed by the following adjective. تنگدل is a compound adjective 'narrow‑hearted / melancholic' and شد is the past of شدن used here in its primary sense 'became' (i.e., 'became narrow‑hearted'). به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly / all at once' with به linking the noun یکبارگی as an adverbial phrase. The clause thus states a sudden change of mood.
segments.s204-g96: که introduces a result or consequence 'so that / such that'. شمشیر is the subject or instrument 'the sword' and زد is the simple past 'struck / struck at'. بر is the preposition 'on / against' and پی here means 'the follower / the one following (i.e., the baggage‑carrier)'; بارگی is a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'burden‑bearing / the burdened one' and completes the object phrase. The clause expresses consequence: because he became narrow‑hearted he struck with his sword at the burden‑bearer.
segments.s204-g185: 'بگفت این' = 'he said this' (verb before omitted subject, common in narrative); 'و برگشت' = 'and he returned' (و = linking 'and/then'); 'و شد باز جای' = 'and became again at his place' where شد is the past of شدن 'became/was' and باز modifies جای as 'again' — the predicate 'شد باز جای' is elliptical for 'he was again in his place.'
segments.s204-g186: This is two parallel verbless clauses with possessive clitics: 'دلش پر ز کین' = 'his heart (دلش) is full (پر) of (ز) hatred/revenge (کین)'; 'سرش پر ز رای' = 'his head is full of plans/counsel (رای)'. The copula 'است/شد' is omitted (typical in poetry). Word order: possessor+clitic after noun (دلش, سرش).
segments.s204-g187: 'برفت او' = 'he departed/went away' (past narrative verb with its pronoun); 'و آمد ز لشکر تژاو' = 'and (there) came from the radiant/bright host' — ز marks source ('from'), and تژاو is an epithet modifying لشکر ('radiant/bright host'). Note: برفت is an archaic/persianized past form (بَرفت/برفت).
segments.s204-g188: 'سواری که بودیش با شیر تاو' is a relative clause modifying 'سواری' — 'a horseman who...'؛ 'که' introduces the clause; 'بودیش' is an archaic/periphrastic past form indicating accompaniment/possession ('had with him' / 'it belonged to him'); 'با' marks accompaniment ('with'), and 'شیر تاو' = 'a lion (شیر) described as تاو (radiant/glowing)'. The clause says the rider was accompanied by a radiant lion.
segments.s204-g189: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'asked of'). پیران is the plural 'elders' (those consulted). بپرسید is the classical past narratorial 'he asked' (prefixed ب-). و is 'and'. پیران بگفت continues the narration: 'and the elders said' (بگفت = 'said'). The verb-subject order is typical of classical narrative.
segments.s204-g190: که introduces the reported speech 'that'. بهرام را uses را to topicalize/mark Bahram ('as for Bahram'). از یلان isn't literal 'from the heroes' but functions as 'among the heroes'. نیست is the copula 'is not/there is not'. جفت here means 'pair/equal/match' — the whole phrase says there is no match for Bahram among the heroes ('Bahram has no equal among the champions'). Note the classical inverted order: predicate (نیست) after the complement.
segments.s204-g191: به مهر‌ش: به + possessive suffix ش = a prepositional phrase meaning 'out of/for his affection' (classical Persian often uses به with abstract nouns to indicate motive). بدادم: an archaic past-1sg form 'I gave' (classical/poetic variant of دادم) placed before the object phrase; here the printed lemma senses do not include the verbal sense, so this is the first-person past. بسی پند خوب: بسی is a quantifier 'much/many' preceding the noun پند 'advice'; خوب 'good' qualifies the advice. Word order: motive phrase → verb → quantified noun phrase.
segments.s204-g192: نمودم بدو راه و پیوند خوب: نمودم is the first-person past of 'to show' (I showed); بدو is the dative 'to him' (به + او contracted to بدو); راه و پیوند خوب lists two nouns joined by و 'and' ('a way and a bond') with خوب 'good' qualifying them — in classical Persian a single adjective can qualify both coordinated nouns. The verb (نمودم) precedes the indirect object and coordinated noun phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s204-g201: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' placed at the clause head. بهرام را دید is a simple past clause (SOV in Persian): proper name + object marker را + past verb دید 'saw'. نیزه به دست is a prepositional/locative phrase 'spear in (his) hand' where به + noun indicates the instrumental/locative relation; this phrase attaches to the main clause to describe Bahram's posture/armament.
segments.s204-g202: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one/someone'; برخروشید is a past-form verb meaning 'roared/rose up' (narrative past). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by پیل مست 'an (intoxicated) elephant', where مست is an adjectival/figurative descriptor. The whole phrase describes the manner of his rising.
segments.s204-g203: بدو گفت is an object-pronoun + verb sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'). ازین لشکر نامدار is a partitive/locative phrase 'from this renowned army/host' with archaic ین as a spelling of این; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' postmodifying لشکر.
segments.s204-g204: پیاده functions as a nominal/qualifier 'on foot' or 'a foot-soldier' and is placed before the numeral phrase. یکی مرد = 'one man' (indefinite), and و چندین سوار coordinates an additional group 'and several horsemen'. Word order is typical Persian: qualifier + numeral + noun, then conjunction + plural group.
segments.s204-g205: This is an idiomatic conditional/taunting line. به ایران is the directional/target phrase 'into Iran'. گرازید is used here as a verbal idea 'to rush/raid' (figurative from 'graz/boar'); خواهی همی is an archaic formula meaning 'if you wish' or 'if you will' that follows the verb in older Persian narratives.
segments.s204-g206: سرت ('your head') is the direct object/topic of the following verb. برفرازید is a causative/intransitive phrase 'raise up/hold high' (literally 'lift upon'), and again خواهی همی repeats the idiomatic 'if you wish / if you will' — a rhetorical repetition that gives the line its taunting, confrontational tone.
segments.s204-g207: سران را سپردی is S (سران) + object marker را + past verb سپردی 'you entrusted/handed over'. سر اندر زمان is a terse idiom meaning 'their heads into (the hands of) time/fate' (سر here echoes 'heads' as the thing given over, اندر زمان = 'to time/fate'), so the clause says you delivered the leaders to their fate.
segments.s204-g208: گه آمد introduces 'an occasion/moment came' (narrative past آمد). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...', and بر تو سرآید زمان literally 'a time should come upon you' (بر = upon, سرآید = 'come/arrive/befall'). The construction predicts that the turn of fate will fall upon the addressed person.
segments.s204-g121: بگفتند is the past plural 'they said' (reported speech marker). کاینت is an archaic demonstrative 'this/that' pointing to the following name; هژبر is a personal name used here; دلیر is an epithet 'brave' modifying the name. The word order places the reporting verb + demonstrative before the name and epithet, introducing quoted or reported comment.
segments.s204-g122: پیاده functions as an adverbial 'on foot / fighting on foot'. نگردد is negative present/subjunctive from گشت/گرد meaning 'does not become / will not be' — here idiomatically 'will not go/serve as (a) footman'. خود is emphatic 'himself'. از introduces the source/thing causing satiety ('from war'), and سیر is the state 'sated / satiated'. Taken together this clause says he will not fight on foot and he himself will not be sated with war.
segments.s204-g123: بپرسید is a past reporting verb 'asked' (they asked). پیران is the agent 'the elders'. که introduces the embedded question 'who is this man' (relative/subordinating particle). این مرد کیست is a straightforward interrogative 'who is this man?' with کی 'who' and ست the copula 'is'.
segments.s204-g124: ازان ('of those' / 'from those') connects him to a class of famed men (نامداران = 'renowned ones'). ورا here functions like classical Persian 'to/for him' (older form of 'او را' or 'نامش'); نام چیست is the formula 'what is (his) name?'. The clause therefore asks both whether he belongs to those illustrious men and asks his name.
segments.s204-g125: یکی گفت is the typical narrative 'someone said' (یکی = 'someone/a certain one'; گفت introduces the quoted identification). بهرام شیراوژن است is the copular identification 'he is Bahram Shirâvozhan' (proper names followed by است).
segments.s204-g126: که here introduces an explanatory relative clause 'whose/that' (rendered 'whose whole army...'). لشکر سراسر = 'the army in its entirety' (سراسر = 'throughout, entire'). بدو means 'to him/around him' and روشن is an adjective 'bright/clear/gleaming'. است is the copula. The clause explains the man's fame by noting his entire host shines about him.
segments.s204-g127: به + رویین addresses someone 'to the iron-clad (one)' (رویین 'iron-clad/armoured' used as epithet). چنین گفت پیران = 'Pīrān thus said' (چنین 'thus/so', گفت 'said', پیران 'the elder/Pīrān' or 'the elders' as speaker). خیز is the imperative 'rise! / advance!'. The clause is an imperative uttered by Pīrān to the iron-clad warrior.
segments.s204-g128: که here gives the reason/explanation 'for/because'. بهرام را uses را as an emphatic/topic marker on the name 'Bahram'. نیست جای گریز = 'there is no place of flight/escape' (نیست 'is not / does not exist', جای 'place', گریز 'escape/flight'). Together: 'for Bahram there is no place to flee.'
segments.s204-g97: وزان جایگه تا بدین رزمگاه = a compact prepositional phrase. وزان is a fused poetic preposition meaning “from that” (و + زان). تا here means “as far as / up to.” The sequence ب + دین represents the directional preposition بدین (بِ + این) = “to/into this,” and رزمگاه is the destination “battlefield.” Word order is source (وزان جایگه) → limit (تا بدین رزمگاه).
segments.s204-g98: پیاده بپیمود چون باد راه = main verb clause. پیاده is an adverbial/qualifier “on foot.” بپیمود is the past verb “he traversed/covered” (the verbal form includes the prefix بـ in classical usage). چون باد راه is a simile: چون + noun “like the wind,” and راه is the object/route ‘the road’—together meaning he covered the way swiftly, “like the wind.”
segments.s204-g99: سراسر همه دشت پرکشته دید = description of extent + observation. سراسر همه (lit. “throughout the whole”) intensifies the scope; دشت is “plain.” پرکشته (a postnominal adjective/compound) means ‘full of the slain’ or 'strewn with corpses.' دید is the past verb “he saw/beheld.” Word order places the extent and noun before the adjectival phrase and final verb, typical of Persian poetic prose.
segments.s204-g100: زمین چون گل و ارغوان کشته دید = a parallel pictorial line. زمین = “the ground/earth” (topic). چون introduces a simile “like”; گل و ارغوان are the images (“flowers and crimson/purple”), and کشته (postnominal adjective or nominal phrase) indicates ‘the slain/corpses’ so that the earth appears like a bed of flowers and crimson because of the dead. The final دید repeats the seeing verb for emphasis; order is topical ground → simile/description → verb.
segments.s204-g101: همی گفت کاکنون چه سازیم روی = speech frame + interrogative. همی + past verb (همی گفت) conveys iterative/continuous past “he kept saying.” کاکنون = “now/at present.” چه سازیم is a deliberative subjunctive/optative: سازیم (1st‑pl. subjunctive/present) here means “shall we do” / “what shall we do.” روی is the noun “face” used with the verb to mean “turn (our) face” or “set (our) face” toward something; together the clause is “he kept saying, ‘Now what shall we do, (shall we) turn our faces...’” Note: گفت appears as the reporting verb and همی marks continuous speech.
segments.s204-g102: بر این دشت بی‌بارگی راه‌جوی = directional phrase plus vocative/address. بر این دشت = “onto / toward this plain” (بر + این + دشت). بی‌بارگی is a postnominal noun/adjective “barrenness,” so دشت بی‌بارگی = “this barren plain.” راه‌جوی at the end is vocative “O wayfarer / traveler,” addressing someone; thus the whole phrase continues the question 'turn our faces toward this barren plain, O traveler?'.
segments.s204-g103: ازو سرکشان آگهی یافتند = report of news being learned. ازو = “from that / from him/it” indicating source. سرکشان is the agent group (likely 'lookouts' or 'those who peered'), here the subject who learns the news; the supplied sense list does not give a direct match so treat as a lexical plural of watchers. آگهی یافتند is a frozen collocation meaning “they received/heard word” or “they obtained information” (یافتند is past 'they found/learned').
segments.s204-g104: سواری صد از قلب بشتافتند = movement clause. سواری صد = “a hundred horsemen” (the numeral follows the noun in this poetic order). از قلب = “from the heart / from the midst” (قلب here = core/midst of the force or place). بشتافتند is the past plural verb “they hurried/rushed.” The phrase therefore means that a hundred riders rushed forth from the center/midst.
segments.s204-g65: سه روزست is a verbless temporal statement meaning “it is/has been three days” (روزست here marks the span of days). تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning “that/since” (classical use close to “that”/“since”); نان و آب are coordinated objects “bread and water”; آرزوست is a predicative nominal: آرزو + است (copula contracted into the poetic -ست) meaning “is a desire/longing.” The whole segment therefore says “it has been three days that bread and water are (my) desire.”
segments.s204-g66: مرا بر یکی جامه خواب آرزوست: مرا (م + را here marks the experiencer/object “me” — poetic cliticization) indicates the person who has the desire; بر functions as a preposition “for/on/toward” governing یکی جامه خواب (“one garment of sleep” — جامه = garment, خواب = sleep); آرزوست again is the predicative nominal “is a longing.” Word order is the older Persian pattern where the possessor/experiencer (مرا) precedes the thing longed for.
segments.s204-g67: بشد تیز بهرام: بشد is the past/preterite form of شدن (“became”); here it describes a change of state — “became/grew.” تیز follows as an adjective meaning “swift/quick.” The subject (بهرام) follows the verb in this inverted/poetic order, so literally “became swift Bahram,” i.e. “Bahram grew swift.” تا پیش اوی introduces the motion/direction “until/up to before him” (تا = until/till, پیش = before/in the presence of, اوی = archaic pronoun “him”).
segments.s204-g68: به دل مهربان و به تن خویش اوی: به + noun is a directional/benefactive phrase “to/into/for the X.” دل مهربان is a genitive phrase “kind heart” (adjective follows noun in Persian poetry). و links the two phrases; به تن خویش اوی literally “to his own body/person” — خویش + ش + اوی here composes the reflexive/possessive notion “his own” in older orthography. Together these two prepositional phrases indicate where Bahram rushed mentally/emotionally (to compassion) and physically (to his person).
segments.s204-g69: برو گشت گریان: برو گشت is a compound verbal phrase in which برو (go) plus گشت (became/turned/went) describes movement away — “he went off/turned and went.” گریان is a present/predicative adjective/participial form meaning “weeping” and describes his manner. و links the next clause; رخ را بخست uses رخ را (face + object marker) and بخست (from بستن, here past tense form meaning “he closed/covered”), i.e. “he covered/closed his face” (an idiom for hiding one’s face).
segments.s204-g70: بدرید پیراهن او را ببست: بدرید is the preterite of دریدن “to tear” meaning “he tore”; پیراهن او را is the direct object “his tunic/shirt” (پیراهن + object marker را); ببست is the preterite of بستن and here means “he tied/bound” (context: tearing and then tying the garment). The sequence shows two successive actions: he tore the tunic and then fastened/tied it.
segments.s204-g71: بدو گفت مندیش کز خستگی‌ست: بدو = ب + دو here is the dative phrase “to him” (to that person); گفت is the past of گفتن “said” (in the word list it is labeled as conversation but here functions as the verb “said”); مندیش (مندی‌ش) is an imperative/negative advisory “do not think” or “do not suppose” (classical/poetic formation); کز = که + ز = relative/conjunction “that/from which” introducing the clause explaining the cause; خستگی‌ست is “is (mere) tiredness” (خستگی + است). The whole line reports speech: “He said to him, ‘Do not suppose that this is from tiredness.’”
segments.s204-g72: تبه بودن این ز نابستگی‌ست: تبه بودن این = “this being ill/this misfortune” (تبه used as noun/adjective ‘evil/misfortune’ + بودن ‘to be’ + این ‘this’); ز here is the poetic preposition “from/because of”; نابستگی‌ست is a nominalised state meaning roughly “separation/detachment” or “not being bound” (نابستگی). The clause therefore states the cause: “this misfortune is because of separation/lack of binding.” The copular -ست marks statement in poetic diction.
segments.s204-g209: 'پس آنگه' are redundant temporal adverbs meaning 'then'/'thereupon'. 'بفرمود' is an honorific past, 'he ordered/commanded'. 'کاندر' is an archaic fused particle (که + اندر) introducing a subordinate clause 'that/in which', and 'نهید' is the verb نهاد in a jussive/subjunctive sense 'to put/place' — together: 'he commanded that (they) be put...'.
segments.s204-g210: The phrase repeats the preposition 'به' for each item as a distributive list marker: 'به تیر و به گرز و به ژوپین' = 'into/with arrows, maces, and jupins (padded jackets)'. 'دهید' is the present/jussive form of دادن used here as the verb of the commanded clause ('give' / 'let them be given'); grammatically it completes the order set up by بفرمود + subordinate clause. 'ژوپین' is an armor term (padded jacket).
segments.s204-g211: 'برو' here functions as a narrative/motion verb 'they went' / 'set off' (imperative sense is possible in isolation but context is narrative). 'انجمن' = 'assembly/gathering' is the noun subject, 'شد' = 'became', 'یکی' = 'one', and 'لشکری' qualifies 'one' as 'one military force' / 'one army' — literal order: 'went; the assembly became one army.'
segments.s204-g212: 'هرانکس که' = 'whoever / anyone who'. 'بد از' is a comparative construction meaning 'worse/less (…) than', where 'بد' has the comparative sense and 'از' marks the standard of comparison. 'دلیران' = 'the brave (men)' and 'سری' = 'head/chief' (here 'سرّی' = leader/head). The phrase thus specifies 'whichever person was inferior to the chiefs among the brave' (i.e., anyone not up to the chiefs' standard). Grammatically it's a relative clause modifying an implied referent in the narrative.
segments.s204-g213: 'کمان را' is the definite object with the object marker را. 'به زه کرد' is a light-verb construction literally 'made (it) to the string' = 'strung/drew the bow' (زه = bowstring). 'بهرام' is the agent/subject. 'گرد' here appears as an epithet/part of the proper-name phrase (or a condensed verb-form 'turned'); in context it groups with the name to present the doer. Word order is object — verbal phrase — subject (a common poetic flexibility).
segments.s204-g214: 'به تیر' indicates the arrow as the instrument/vehicle; 'از هوا' = 'from the air/heavens' (source). 'روشنایی' = 'light/brightness' is the object carried, and 'ببرد' (برد + ب- prefix) is a past/jussive form meaning 'took/carried away' (here poetically 'the arrow took light from the air' = 'it flashed like light'). Word order places the verb at the end, typical of Persian narrative clauses.
segments.s204-g215: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; تیر is the subject 'the arrow'. اسپری does not match a clear classical sense here (likely an archaic or scribal form), so its precise role is uncertain in this clause. شد is a past-form 'became/was' and سوی نیزه is a prepositional phrase 'toward the spear'; گشت is an intransitive past verb 'turned/changed', so the clause describes the arrow being sent and turning toward the spear.
segments.s204-g216: چو again introduces a temporal clause 'when/as'; دریای خون شد is a predicative phrase 'became a sea of blood' (metaphorical result). همه کوه و دشت is a postverbal affected noun phrase 'the whole mountain and plain' (poetic inversion places the affected grounds after the verb), and و is the simple conjunction 'and'. The construction implies that mountain and plain were turned into or covered by a sea of blood.
segments.s204-g137: یکی here means 'one' or 'a certain one' (an unspecified person) and functions as the subject of the clause. تیرباران is a noun 'a shower/volley of arrows' and is the object of the action. به introduces the target/direction ('at/toward') and رویین is a descriptive noun/adjective 'iron, iron‑hard' referring to armour; بکرد is the simple past (3rd‑person) 'did/made' indicating that this person caused the volley to fall on the iron. Word order is subject — object — prepositional target — verb (typical in older Persian narrative).
segments.s204-g138: که here introduces the result/clause 'that/and then', linking the previous action to its visual effect. شد is the past of شدن 'became'. ماه is 'moon' used metaphorically for a shining disc (often a helmet or shield surface in poetry); تابنده is the present participle/adjective 'shining'. چون is a simile particle 'like/as', and لاژورد is 'lapis‑lazuli' (a blue/azure color). The clause therefore states the resultant appearance (became like lapis‑lazuli).
segments.s204-g139: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. رویین modifies پیران (an attributive adjective + noun sequence) meaning 'iron(‑clad) elders' or 'the elders' iron' depending on context; here it describes an iron object associated with پیران. ز indicates instrument/agent 'by/from' (poetic 'by'), تیرش is 'his arrow' (تیر + possessive), and بخست is the past form of بستن used here in the sense 'was struck/closed' (poetic usage: a past action affecting the iron). The clause is a temporal subordinate ('when ...').
segments.s204-g140: یلان را: یلان 'heroes' with را marking them as the (definite) affected group. همه 'all' emphasizes totality. کند شد is a two‑word predicate where کند (here adjectival/participial in older usage) conveys 'dull/weak/sluggish' and شد 'became' makes it past passive/resultative — 'became weak/dulled'. پای و دست 'feet and hands' are the affected body parts. The order is topic (heroes) — quantifier — predicate — result (body parts).
segments.s204-g141: به سستی is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in/with weakness' (به + noun), where به is used adverbially ('-ly/by'). بر پهلوان آمدند is 'they came upon/against the champion' (بر = upon; آمدند = past plural 'they came'). The clause describes the manner and target of their movement.
segments.s204-g142: پر از درد is a frozen adjectival phrase 'full of pain' (پر = full, از = 'of/from', درد = pain). و connects the second descriptor تیره‌روان 'gloomy/ill‑spirited' and آمدند repeats the verb 'they came' for poetic emphasis. The second آمدند is a resumptive/repeated verb common in epic style to stress their arrival in that emotional state.
segments.s204-g143: که introduces the clause ('that/for'); هرگز is an adverb 'never'. چنین functions as 'such' modifying the following noun phrase. یک پیاده is 'a single foot‑soldier/infantryman' (یک = one/a, پیاده = on foot). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in/for battle' (به + جنگ = 'in battle'). The clause asserts that such a thing has never been seen.
segments.s204-g144: ز دریا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the sea' (ز = from, دریا = sea) specifying the battle's origin. ندیدیم is first‑person plural past 'we did not see'. جنگی functions adjectivally 'warlike/war‑sized' modifying نهنگ (here a metaphor); نهنگ literally 'whale' but used metaphorically for something huge. Together they form a poetic hyperbole — 'a whale of a battle' — emphasizing the enormous, sea‑borne nature of the fight.
segments.s204-g33: دگر پنج دارم همه زرنگار = 'دگر' before a numeral means 'another / more' so 'دگر پنج' = 'five more'; 'دارم' is 1st-person present stem 'I have / I possess'; 'همه' follows the verb and refers back to the items ('all of them'); 'زرنگار' is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'gold-embroidered / brocaded' describing those five.
segments.s204-g34: برو بافته گوهر شاهوار = this is an adjectival/participial string describing the same items: 'بافته' is the past participle 'woven'; 'گوهر' here is a modifier 'of jewels / gem-like (i.e. jeweled)'; 'شاهوار' is an adjective 'regal, stately'. The initial 'برو' (literal sense 'go') appears in the verse as a vocative/poetic marker addressing the gifts (a common epic device) and in effect links the adjectival phrase to the preceding noun phrase: '(they are) (go-)woven with jewels, kingly.'
segments.s204-g35: ترا بخشم این هفت ز ایدر مرو = 'ترا' (the clitic t + را) marks the indirect object 'to you'; 'بخشم' is from بخشیدن, here 1st-person present/future sense 'I grant / I will give'; 'این هفت' 'these seven' is the thing given; 'ز' = 'from'; 'ایدر' = 'here'; 'مرو' is the prohibitive/imperative 'do not go' (classical poetic usage), so the clause ends with the admonition 'do not leave here (for Merv)'.
segments.s204-g36: یکی جنگ خیره میارای نو = 'یکی' is the numeral/indefinite 'one' (a single); 'جنگ' = 'battle/war'; 'خیره' here is an attributive adjective meaning 'dazzling / astonishing' (not literal 'staring'); 'میارای' is the present-tense verb from ārāstan 'to adorn/prepare' and functions as an imperative/optative here ('bring/prepare'); 'نو' = 'new' modifies 'جنگ'. The whole is an exhortation to bring/prepare one fresh, splendid battle.
segments.s204-g37: چنین گفت با گیو بهرام گرد = 'چنین' = 'thus/so' frames the reported speech; 'گفت' is the past tense 'he said' (reporting verb); 'با' here introduces the person addressed/associated ('to/with'); 'گیو' is the proper name Giv; 'بهرام گرد' is the speaker's name (Bahrām‑gerd), placed in the line as subject/agent. Word order is typical of epic poetry: framing adverb + reporting verb + accompaniment + name.
segments.s204-g38: که این ننگ را خرد نتوان شمرد = 'که' introduces the reported clause ('that'); 'این ننگ را' is the object 'this shame' (را marks the definite object); 'خرد' = 'wisdom, reason' used instrumentally ('by wisdom'); 'نتوان' is the negative potential 'cannot'; 'شمرد' (infinitive/root form) = 'to count / reckon'. Together: 'that this shame cannot be counted (measured) by wisdom.'
segments.s204-g39: شما را ز رنگ و نگار‌ست گفت = 'شما را' is topical/accusative 'as for you' (را marks the person being spoken of); 'ز' = 'from / about / on account of'; 'رنگ و نگارست' is a poetic fused predicate meaning 'color and ornament (are yours) / you are of color and adornment' (نگارست here is an adjectival/predicative form, 'is ornamented'); 'گفت' is the reporting verb 'they said' (i.e. people said of you). The clause reports praise of the addressee's appearance.
segments.s204-g40: مرا آنک شد نام با ننگ جفت = 'مرا' ('for me' / 'to me') marks the experiencer; 'آنک' = relative/pointing 'that / what (was said or happened)'; 'شد' is the past 'became'; 'نام' 'my name / reputation' is the subject that follows the verb in poetic order; 'با ننگ جفت' = 'paired with shame' where 'جفت' = 'paired/joined'. The line says: 'for me it happened that my name became yoked to shame.'
segments.s204-g145: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a past‑tense verb 'heard' (3rd person) placed before its subject (پیران) — a common literary inversion. غمی گشت سخت is a second clause: غمی is the subject 'sorrow', گشت is the past of شدن 'became', and سخت is a predicate adjective 'severe/strong', so together 'sorrow became severe.'
segments.s204-g146: بلرزید is a simple past intransitive 'trembled/was shaken' (3rd person). برسان here functions with a causative/affective force tied to the following noun phrase (برگ درخت) — in effect 'caused/affected the leaves of the tree' — so the whole phrase reads 'the leaves of the tree trembled.' برگ درخت is the noun phrase 'the leaves of the tree' (leaf + genitive noun).
segments.s204-g147: نشست is a past verb 'sat/settled/withdrew' (3rd person) used here of a person removing himself from something. از بر is a classical two‑part prepositional expression 'from/away from (upon)', and باره تند تاز is a noun phrase: باره (here 'burst/assault') modified by the paired adjectives تند تاز 'swift, fierce' — together 'the fierce onrush/assault.' Word order and paired adjectives are literary.
segments.s204-g148: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/habitual past form 'was going/went' (literary continuous past). با او is the preposition + pronoun 'with him.' بسی here functions as a quantifier 'many/much' in classical usage, followed by رزمساز 'warrior/one who makes battle' — together 'many warriors accompanied him.'
segments.s204-g149: بیامد is a past verb 'came' (3rd person). بدو is the clitic form 'to him/unto him' marking the direction/patient of the approach. گفت is the past verb 'said' (3rd person) introducing quoted speech; note the lemma senses listed are nominal but here it functions as the verb 'he said'. کای is the vocative particle 'O', and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/illustrious' addressing the addressee.
segments.s204-g150: پیاده is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'on foot' (i.e. fighting or going on foot). چرا is the interrogative 'why'. ساختی is the past 2nd‑person singular of ساختن/ساختن used here as 'you made/you undertook' (you did). کارزار is the object 'battle/struggle' — so the clause asks 'Why did you undertake the battle on foot?' with verb before object in literary word order.
segments.s204-g151: This clause begins with the negative particle (implicit in the line) and then تو 'you' as subject. با سیاوش is a prepositional phrase 'with Siawush' (marking the person concerned). به توران is the directional/recipient phrase 'to Turan.' بدی here corresponds to a classical past 2nd‑person form meaning 'you gave/handed over' (historical orthography), so the clause reads 'you (did) give Siawush to Turan' (negated by the earlier negative). The lemma senses listed do not include this verbal sense, so that sense is marked below as unavailable.
segments.s204-g152: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed.' به introduces the indirect object(s) 'to' or 'into' and governs the following nouns: پرخاش 'reproach/insult' and سوران 'revellers/feasters' joined by و 'and.' بدی is the same past 2nd‑person verb 'you gave/handed over' as in the previous clause; here the sense is 'you handed him over to reproach and to revellers.' The verbal sense is not in the provided sense list and is marked accordingly.
segments.s204-g153: The phrase uses the infinitive خوردن ('to eat') plus the copula است to state an existential/nomominal fact: 'to eat (bread and salt) with you (exists for me)' — idiomatically 'I have shared bread and salt with you' (i.e. we are bound by hospitality). مـ + را here represent the object clitic (مـ = me, را = object marker) attached before the prepositional phrase با تو; word order places the object clitic before the prepositional phrase and the verb infinitive, and است closes the clause as a copula.
segments.s204-g154: نشستن is an infinitive/gerund 'sitting' (here 'sitting together'); همان introduces an identity/equivalence ('the same as'), and مهر پروردن is a light verb construction meaning 'to foster/cultivate affection' (مهر = love/affection; پروردن = to cultivate). The clause ends with the copula است; the construction equates the activity of sitting together with fostering affection.
segments.s204-g155: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / one must not'; که introduces the subordinate clause. با این نژاد و گهر is a prepositional phrase ('with this lineage and jewel') acting as the complement/setting for whatever action is being condemned; note the poetic noun pair نژاد (lineage) and گهر (jewel/precious thing).
segments.s204-g156: This tokenization splits what functions in the text as بدین ('to/for such') into ب (a prefix/preposition 'to/into') + دین (here a tokenization artifact — دین does not mean 'religion' in context). شیرمردی is a noun meaning 'heroism' (or 'brave man' if used personally), and چندین هنر = 'several/many arts/skills' (چندین quantifies هنر). The whole phrase expresses that one should not apply (or treat) that lineage/gem by/with such heroics and many skills.
segments.s204-g157: This is a classical, somewhat inverted conditional protasis: ز = 'from', بالا = 'on high', به خاک = 'to the earth/ground', اندر آید = 'falls into / comes into' (اندر is an archaic preposition 'into'), and سرت ('your head') is the affected noun placed at the end for emphasis. The verb آید is present/optative in form but here functions as the protasis verb 'should fall / comes to fall'.
segments.s204-g158: بسوزد is a jussive/subjunctive third-person verb 'may ... burn' or 'let ... be consumed' (a curse/wish-construction). دل مهربان مادرت is the subject/affected phrase 'the kind heart of your mother', where مادرت = مادر + -ت (second-person possessive suffix). The clause thus wishes that the mother's kind heart be consumed (i.e. grievously pained).
segments.s204-g159: بیا is an imperative/hortative 'come' used here as an invitation ('let us'); تا introduces purpose ('so that / in order that') and governs the subjunctive بسازیم ('let us make' — 1st person plural subjunctive). سوگند و بند are the objects 'oath and bond' (بند here = bond/fetter, not 'stanza'), coordinated by و.
segments.s204-g160: براهی ('in the way' / 'in whatever manner') is an adverbial phrase; که introduces the relative clause 'that', آید ('comes') is the verb, and دلت را (دل + -ت + object marker را) marks 'your heart' as the entity whose approval matters. پسـند is 'liking/approval' — the clause literally says 'that comes to be your heart's liking' i.e. 'that pleases your heart'. Note the idiomatic object marking دلت را before the final predicate پسند.
segments.s204-g199: بیامد is a past-tense simple verb ('came'/'hurried in'), here poetic for آمد; شتابان is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'hastily/quickly' modifying the verb. بدان is a prepositional phrase formed from the prefix ب- ('to/into') + a demonstrative element (here meaning 'that'), so بدان رزمگاه = 'to that battlefield'; word order is verb + adverb + prepositional destination, typical of poetry.
segments.s204-g200: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where' introducing the question; بود is the past-tense copula 'was' (not an auxiliary here). بهرام is the proper name (subject/topic) and یل is an appositive epithet 'the hero/valiant one' following the name; بی‌سپاه is a postpositive adjective/phrase 'without an army' describing him. The clause order (question word + verb + name + epithet + modifier) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s204-g169: بدو (be-do) is the prepositional pronoun meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (verb before subject here, a common classical order); پیران is the speaker's name/title (Piran) used as the grammatical subject. که‌ای نامجوی is a vocative: که (here introducing/exclaiming) + ای (vocative) attached to نامجوی 'name-seeker' (addressing the addressee).
segments.s204-g170: ندانی is the 2nd-person present (subjunctive/indicative) negative/form used interrogatively meaning 'do you not know'; که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; این رای را is the definite object ('this counsel/opinion' with را as the object marker); نیست is the copula 'is not/there is not'; روی literally 'face/standing' and here idiomatically means 'standing/legitimacy' (i.e., 'has no standing').
segments.s204-g171: ترا (here written as ت + را) is the old form of 'to you' (ت = second-person stem, را = object/indirect-object marker); این به آید is an impersonal phrasing 'this may come/happen' or 'you may think this'; که introduces the clause that follows; گفتم سخن is 'I said a word' (گفتم = I said, سخن = 'speech/word'), object placed after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s204-g172: دلیری is a noun meaning 'bravery/valor' (here qualifying the 'word' just mentioned). و is the simple conjunction 'and/but'; بر is the preposition 'against/toward'; خیره is an adjective/noun 'astonished, stupefied' (the person toward whom the warning is addressed); تندی means 'haste/fierceness' and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. Together بر خیره تندی مکن = 'do not be hasty/act rashly toward the bewildered.'
segments.s204-g173: ببین is the imperative 'look/see' (command to observe or ensure); تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; سواران آن انجمن is the subject phrase 'the horsemen of that assembly' (سواران = horsemen/riders, آن انجمن = that assembly).
segments.s204-g174: نهند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form of نهاد 'to place/set' (used here in a purposive clause with تا: 'so that they place/so that they may not place'); این‌چنین ننگ بر خویشتن is the object phrase 'such a shame upon themselves' (این‌چنین = 'thus/such', ننگ = 'shame', بر = 'on/upon', خویشتن = 'themselves'). The whole phrase means 'do not let them bring such disgrace upon themselves.'
segments.s204-g175: که here resumes/explains (’for/that’); چندین تن is a quantitative phrase 'several persons/men' (چندین = several, تن = persons); از تخمه مهتران means 'from the seed/lineage (تخمه) of the مهتران' — i.e., 'from the clan/line of the chiefs/elders.'
segments.s204-g176: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دیهیم داران is a compound: دیهیم (rural, villager) + داران (holders/possessors), so 'village-holders' or 'rural landowners'; و is 'and'; کنداوران (plural) are 'those who dig/excavate' (here likely a social/occupational group). The string lists the groups from which those several men come.
segments.s204-g129: مگر introduces a conditional with the sense 'unless'. زنده 'alive' modifies او (here the person to be captured). را is the definite-object marker attached to او. به + چنگ آوری is a verbal phrase: چنگ 'claw/grasp' + آوری (from آوَرْدن) is a 2nd/3rd-person verbal form meaning 'bring/bring about' or here 'seize/take' — in poetry آوری functions as the subjunctive/imperative sense 'you seize' or generic 'one seizes'. Word order is conditional phrase first (مگر ...) marking what must happen for the following clause to be averted.
segments.s204-g130: زمانه is the subject ('time' / 'fortune'). براساید is an archaic/poetic verbal form (third-person, with the sense ‘befit/it suits’ or more loosely 'let/it will') — here it functions as the main verb meaning roughly 'let Time act' or 'Time will (do)'. از + داوری is a prepositional phrase 'from/of judgment' specifying what Time acts with respect to. The clause therefore says what happens if the preceding condition is not met.
segments.s204-g131: ز (poetic) = 'from'. لشکر is the noun 'army/ranks'. کسی 'someone/anyone' is the object marked by را. که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. باید is a modal 'must/ought to' and is followed by the verb ببر in the imperative/ hortative sense 'take!' (here a command or strong suggestion). Word order: prepositional phrase + object + relative clause + modal + verb (imperative).
segments.s204-g132: کجا is an interrogative 'where'. نامدار‌ست is a predicative adjective + copula 'is famed/renowned' (name + -dar + -ast). و is the coordinating 'and'. پرخاشخر is an adjective/noun meaning 'aggressive, quarrelsome' describing the same referent; in poetic usage it comments on character. The clause is a rhetorical question about the person's fame and hot temper.
segments.s204-g133: چو here = 'when' (a temporal conjunction). بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard'. رویین is the proper name (written same as the adjective 'iron-like/iron-clad' in classical Persian) and functions as the subject of بیامد ('came'). بیامد is past tense 'came'. دمان here appears as a noun (poetic/place/name); in sequence it follows the verb as a circumstantial/locative element (poetic word order often permits this placement).
segments.s204-g134: نبودش is a fused negative+pronoun meaning 'he did not have' / 'there was not to him'. بس is an adverb 'much/enough' modifying اندیشه. اندیشه 'thought' is the noun; بدگمان 'suspicious/distrustful' qualifies اندیشه. The whole phrase says he lacked abundant suspicious thoughts (i.e., he was not much distrustful).
segments.s204-g135: بر + تیر is a locative phrase 'on the shaft/arrow' (بر = on). بنشست is past tense 'sat down' (poetic past). بهرام is the proper name (subject). شیر follows as an epithet 'the Lion' describing Bahram; in Persian epithets commonly follow the name. Word order here places the locative first for poetic effect.
