segments.s204-g136: نهاده is a perfect/participle 'having set/placed' (here a circumstantial participle giving attendant circumstance). سپر 'shield' is its object. بر سر = 'upon (his) head' (بر = on, سر = head). و coordinates the final phrase. چرخ زیر = 'the sky beneath' (چرخ = sky/heaven, زیر = under/beneath); together the participle clause paints the posture: shield on head and the sky below (poetic image).
segments.s204-g57: بران is a literary/archaic particle meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'on that', introducing what follows; کشتگان ('the slain') is the noun phrase subject; بر یکایک is a prepositional/adverbial grouping meaning 'one by one' (بر here reinforcing locative/iterative sense); بگشت is an older past form meaning 'turned/returned' (here describing the motion or disposition of the slain). Word order is typical older Persian: particle → subject → adverbial → verb.
segments.s204-g58: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause ('for/which'), بودند is the past plural of 'to be' ('were'), افگنده is a past participle meaning 'cast' or 'thrown' describing the state of the slain, بر is the preposition 'on/upon', and پهن‌دشت is a compound noun 'broad plain' giving the location. The clause therefore explains where/how they lay.
segments.s204-g59: ازان is a genitive/demonstrative 'of those' (literally 'from those'), naming the group; نامداران is the plural 'renowned ones' (i.e. famed heroes); یکی is an indefinite 'one' or 'a certain (one)'; خسته بود is the adjectival predicate 'was tired/exhausted' (خسته + past copula بود). Word order: prepositional/genitive phrase → head noun → numeral/pronoun → adjective → copula.
segments.s204-g60: This clause gives the cause/condition: به شمشیر uses به plus instrument to mean 'by the sword'; ازیشان (= ازِ ایشان) indicates relation to 'them' (here 'by/from them'); به جان رسته بود is an idiomatic phrasing: جان 'life' and رسته (past participle of رستن) 'freed/loosed' together with بود 'was' expresses that his life had been released (i.e. he had been put to death or fatally wounded). The sequence is instrumental phrase → agentive phrase → resultant state.
segments.s204-g61: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in classical Persian, here marking a prompt or continuous action; بازدانست is a compound verb (باز- + دانست) meaning 'to check/hold back' (past tense 'he checked/held back'); بهرام را uses the object marker را to mark 'Bahram' as the object of بازدانست. The whole means 'he held Bahram back' or 'he checked Bahram.'
segments.s204-g62: This is two coordinated verbs: بنالید is a past-tense verb 'he moaned/uttered a lament'; و 'and' connects it to پرسید 'he asked' (past). The two short words ز and و here combine as ز + او = 'from him' (poetic contraction), so پرسید زو نام را = 'he asked from him the name' (i.e. 'he asked him his name'). Note the classical order places پرسید before the object phrase with را.
segments.s204-g63: بدو ('to him') is the prepositional dative 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical past third-person); کای is a vocative particle 'O'; شیر من 'my lion' is an affectionate or martial address; زنده‌ام is the present/perfect 'I am alive' (زنده + enclitic أم). The clause is direct speech introduced by 'he said to him'.
segments.s204-g64: بر کشتگان is a locative phrase 'upon the slain'; خوار is an adjective 'despised, lowly' modifying the speaker's state; افگنده‌ام is perfective 'I have been cast' or 'I lie cast' (افگنده with first-person enclitic ام). Grammatically this is a predicative clause describing how he lies among the dead.
segments.s204-g105: که is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'so that / that' (introducing purpose/result). او را is the object with the definite object marker را. بگیرند is a 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative (classical/poetic form) meaning 'they seize' or 'may they seize'; the subject is omitted and shared with the next verb. زان رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' (زان = 'from that', رزمگاه = 'battlefield') placed after the verb.
segments.s204-g106: برندش is a verb (برند) with the enclitic object pronoun -ش 'him' = 'they carry/take him'. بر is the preposition 'to/toward' introducing the recipient. پهلوان سپاه is a genitival/epithetic sequence 'the champion of the host' (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry), so the whole phrase means 'to the army's champion.'
segments.s204-g107: کمان را is the definite object 'the bow' (را marks it). به زه کرد literally 'made (put) the string to it' — here کرد (past 'did/made') means 'he strung' or 'he drew' the bow; به (to) + زه (the bowstring) expresses the action of setting the string to the bow. The subject appears after the verb as an epithet: بهرام شیر 'Bahram the Lion' (name + epithet). The order (object – verb – subject) is typical of epic poetry.
segments.s204-g108: ببارید is a past intransitive verb 'it/they fell/rained' used metaphorically here ('arrows rained'). تیر is the (grammatical) subject 'arrows'. از کمان دلیر = 'from the brave one's bow' (از = 'from', کمان = 'bow', دلیر = 'the brave' modifying the bow; ezāfe omitted). The clause describes arrows falling out of the brave's bow.
segments.s204-g109: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. تیری یکی = 'one arrow' (تيري = 'arrow' with the possessive/relational form; یکی = 'one/a single'). در کمان is the locative 'in the bow'. راندی is a past tense form of راندن 'to drive/insert' (classical/poetic ending -ی); in Ferdowsi this form functions as a past action 'he drove/placed (the arrow)'. Word order places the verb finally/after the phrase in epic style.
segments.s204-g110: به پیرامنش is a locative phrase 'at/around its rim' (پیرامن + enclitic -ش 'its' = 'around it'). کس is the indefinite pronoun 'anyone/someone' (here rhetorical), کجا is the interrogative 'where', and ماندی is the past of ماندن 'to remain' (final verb); together they form a rhetorical question 'who could remain where?' referring to the space at the bow's rim.
segments.s204-g111: ازیشان ('از' + ایشان) means 'from them'. فراوان is an adverb 'in great number/abundantly' modifying the verbs. بخست و بکشت are two past verbs joined by و: poetic/archaic past forms meaning 'he smote/struck down and he killed' (paratactic sequence). These are classical/epic verb forms; because the provided sense list doesn't give 'kill/strike' exactly, the precise lexical senses are idiomatic here.
segments.s204-g112: پیاده here denotes the foot soldiers/infantry (noun use). نپیچید is the negative past of پیچیدن 'to turn/retreat' (ن- prefix negates the verb): 'did not turn'. و connects the clauses. ننمود is the negative past of نمودن 'to show/appear' ('did not show'). پشت is 'back' (body/rear) — 'did not show their back' = they did not flee/turn their backs. The segment praises the infantry's steadfastness.
segments.s204-g161: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (ازان = 'from that/thereupon', پس = 'then'). یکی introduces a single act ('one thing' / 'let us do one thing') and is followed by با تو خویشی کنیم, where خویشی ('kinship') is the object and کنیم is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative meaning 'let us make' or 'we should make'. Word order is verb‑final for the clause (object + کنیم).
segments.s204-g162: چو here means 'when/if.' خویشی is the same noun 'kinship' and بود is the copula 'is/was' indicating existence ('when/if there is kinship'). رای بیشی کنیم is a verbal phrase: رای = 'counsel/plan', بیشی = 'greater/more', and کنیم is again the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative 'we should take/do' (i.e. 'we will take greater counsel' or 'let us take fuller counsel').
segments.s204-g163: This is a descriptive phrase: پیاده 'on foot' (or 'foot‑soldier'), followed by the pronoun تو 'you' and a prepositional/associative phrase با لشکری نامدار 'with/against a renowned army'. Adjective نامدار follows لشکری as is normal in Persian. The whole phrase sets up the situation addressed to 'you'.
segments.s204-g164: نتابی is n‑ (negative) + تابِی (2nd person singular of تابیدن here meaning 'to endure/withstand'), so 'you will not/cannot withstand.' مخور is a negative imperative 'do not take/do not risk' (here idiomatic: 'do not risk/venture'), باتنت = با + تنت 'with your body/your person', and زینهار is an interjection 'beware/take heed.' Together: 'You cannot withstand a famed army; do not risk your person — beware.'
segments.s204-g165: بدو is the dative/locative clitic 'to him' (i.e. 'to him' or 'unto him'), and گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (3rd person). The sequence 'بدو گفت بهرام' = 'Bahram said to him.' کای is the vocative particle 'O,' addressing the following noun پهلوان 'champion/hero'.
segments.s204-g166: A string of epithets applied to the addressee: خردمند 'wise', و 'and', بینا 'discerning/sighted', و 'and', روشن‌روان 'bright‑soul / clear‑spirited'. They are coordinate adjectives following the vocative and characterise the person being addressed.
segments.s204-g167: مرا حاجت از تو = 'my need/requirement from you' (مرا here marks the experiencer/possessor: 'for me / my'). یکی بارگی‌ست is a predicative phrase: یکی 'one' and بارگی‌ست 'is a …' (here 'one thing/one request' in sense). The copula is implicit in the older form -ست. Overall: 'My need from you is one thing.' Note: older Persian uses مرا fronted to mark the person concerned.
segments.s204-g168: وگر نه = 'and/but if not' (i.e. 'if not'), مرا جنگ یکبارگی‌ست = 'for me, war is a one‑time occurrence.' Here مرا again marks the experiencer/possessor, جنگ = 'war/battle', and یکبارگی‌ست 'is a single occurrence / one‑off' (یکبارگی = 'single occurrence' + ـست copula). The sense: 'If you will not grant that one thing, then for me war will be a one‑time event.'
segments.s204-g193: سخن را: the object 'the speech/words' marked by را (definite/object marker). نبد is an archaic negative/existential verb ('there was not' / 'did not find') placed after the object (poetic order). بر دلش 'upon his heart' is the locative phrase showing where no entrance was found; هیچ راه 'no way' functions as the logical predicate ('no way [exists] into his heart').
segments.s204-g194: همی راه جوید: همی is an adverb 'still/continually' that emphasizes the ongoing action; راه جوید is present/poetic third-person singular 'he seeks/searches for a way' (جوید from جستن). به ایران سپاه: a prepositional phrase 'for/into Iran' followed by سپاه 'army' — the intended meaning is 'he seeks a way to bring an army into Iran'; classical word order allows the locative (به ایران) and the thing (سپاه) to appear in this sequence.
segments.s204-g195: به پیران چنین گفت: به پیران is the indirect object 'to the elders'; چنین گفت is the fixed expression 'said thus' (چنین + گفت). جنگی تژاو is a vocative or epithet: جنگی 'warlike' (adjective) precedes تژاو (a proper/name or epithet meaning 'radiant' — here used as an address). Note that the printed lemma for گفت lists noun senses (conversation), but here it is the verb 'he said'.
segments.s204-g196: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. با مهر جان is an instrumental/manner phrase 'by/with the love/affection of (one's) life' (با + مهر + جان). ترا را is the emphatic/marked object 'you' (ت + را = ترا را, archaic doubling of object marking for emphasis). نیست is the copular negative 'is not' and تاو is the predicate (here 'that radiance/glow'); the clause therefore states that that radiance is not yours even by the love of your life. Word order places the negated copula before the predicate noun (typical of older Persian poetic style).
segments.s204-g197: شوم‌گر is an epithet/conditional-style word meaning 'ill-omened' or 'doer of harm' (here functioning as a conditional/characterizing word: 'if I/being the ill-doer...'). پیاده 'on foot' modifies the manner of the action. به + چنگ is instrumental 'by/with the grasp (claw/hand)'; چنگ here means 'claw' or 'grasp' and is the instrument. آرمش appears at the end as the action 'I will (cause to) rest / I will place' — in the manuscript this form is poetic/archaic and the listed lemma senses are nominal ('rest'), so the verbal sense is irregular/archaic here (hence interpreted as a verb meaning 'I will put/seize/lay to rest').
segments.s204-g198: سر اندر زمان: سر 'head' appears fronted as the direct object; اندر زمان is a time/place phrase 'in/at the time' or 'soon' (classical اندر = in/within). زیر سنگ آرمش: زیر سنگ 'under a stone' is the locative/instrumental phrase and آرمش again closes the clause — here used as a verb-like action 'I will make rest' or 'I will lay (him) to rest under a stone.' The order (object → temporal phrase → locative → verb) is idiomatic in older/epic Persian.
